source.title: Serotoninergic regulation of emotional and behavioural control processes

confidence_rationale: High-quality review from a leading group (Robbins lab), directly addresses 5-HT modulation of aversive processing and response inhibition in PFC. The two-process framework (aversion sensitivity vs. response inhibition) maps cleanly onto the distinct failure modes of MECH-188. Transfer risk is moderate: this review synthesises rodent and human data but does not directly measure PFC working memory maintenance of goal representations during harm spikes -- it addresses punishment sensitivity rather than goal persistence per se.
failure_signatures: 5-HT depletion enhances aversive processing and disinhibits punished behaviours -- predicts failure mode where harm spikes displace goals
5-HT promotes response inhibition -- maps to the PFC mechanism that prevents z_harm_a from overwriting z_goal
Exaggerated aversive processing + deficient response inhibition underlie affective disorder symptoms -- aligns with MECH-188 failure phenomenology (cannot-hold-a-plan state)

mapping.source_claim_statement: 5-HT modulates the impact of punishment-related signals on learning and emotion (aversion), and also promotes response inhibition. Exaggerated aversive processing and deficient response inhibition underlie stress/threat vulnerability and compulsive behaviour respectively.
mapping.ree_translation: In REE terms: 5-HT acting in PFC controls two separable failure modes of MECH-188. (1) Aversion sensitivity failure: low 5-HT causes z_harm_a spikes to have disproportionate weight, collapsing z_goal even when the goal representation is otherwise viable. (2) Response inhibition failure: low 5-HT permits pre-potent harm-avoidance responses to gate out goal-directed action, even when z_goal is maintained internally. MECH-188 requires adequate 5-HT tone to resist both failure modes -- maintaining z_goal representation AND preventing harm-avoidance behaviour from preempting goal execution.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper frames 5-HT effects largely in terms of punishment learning and behavioural flexibility, not working memory maintenance per se. The mapping to z_goal persistence requires one inferential step: PFC 5-HT that suppresses aversive processing is the same substrate that maintains goal representations against aversive interruption. This is plausible given PFC layer V pyramidal cell 5-HT2A receptor anatomy, but not directly tested in this review.
mapping.source_context: The paper was written in 2007-08, before optogenetic circuit-level dissection was available. It synthesises lesion, depletion, and pharmacological studies. The Robbins lab framing is influential and widely cited -- this is a foundational review for the 5-HT/PFC/behavioural-control literature.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high (Trends CogSci, Robbins lab, 800+ citations). Mapping fidelity moderate -- the review covers the right circuit but the dependent variable is punishment sensitivity/response inhibition rather than goal-state maintenance under harm. Transfer risk modest -- the mechanistic claims are about 5-HT in PFC circuits, which is directly relevant, but the operationalisation differs from what MECH-188 predicts.
